skill.name: pdf
skill.description: PDF processing toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating documents, merging or splitting files, converting pages to images, and filling PDF forms.
skill.summary: Process, inspect, create, combine, convert, and fill PDF documents with guided local workflows.
skill.author: ComposioHQ

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no evidence of malware, prompt injection, credential theft, or data exfiltration. Most hits are false positives from Markdown code fences, PDF coordinate arrays, test fixtures, sample passwords, and documentation URLs. The remaining legitimate risk is that helper scripts and documented workflows read and write local user-selected files and may invoke external PDF tools.

medium_findings.title: Local file read and write helpers
medium_findings.description: The Python helper scripts intentionally read PDFs, JSON files, and images from CLI arguments and write output PDFs, JSON, PNG files, or validation images. This is expected for a PDF skill and no exfiltration was found, but users should run it only on intended paths because outputs can overwrite local files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file reads and writes are present in executable scripts and are tied to user-provided paths. Confidence is high for local filesystem impact, but no malicious destination or hidden exfiltration path was found.

medium_findings.title: External PDF tool workflows documented
medium_findings.description: The skill documentation instructs agents to use command-line PDF tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, pdfimages, and helper scripts. These are legitimate PDF operations, but external command execution should preserve argument boundaries and avoid shell interpolation of untrusted filenames.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line ranges clearly document external command usage, but they are not code that builds shell commands dynamically. Risk depends on the calling agent and runtime environment.

low_findings.title: Markdown backtick detections are documentation false positives
low_findings.description: The numerous static hits for Ruby or shell backtick execution are caused by Markdown inline code spans and fenced examples. Review found normal PDF examples rather than executable Ruby backticks or dynamic shell construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged files are Markdown documentation, and the representative lines are code fences or inline examples. No Ruby execution context exists in these files.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are mostly PDF coordinates and sample password examples
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto findings matched numeric PDF bounding boxes, test fixture arrays, and sample password-protection snippets. No custom cryptographic implementation, weak hash use, or credential handling logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are coordinate lists or PDF password examples. They do not show MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic primitives being implemented by the skill.

low_findings.title: C2 keyword detections are terminology false positives
low_findings.description: Static C2 keyword hits occur in benign PDF processing examples, such as document merge commands and field or file names. No beaconing, persistence, remote control channel, or outbound callback code was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is PDF merging and page selection documentation. Network searches in the repository found only license and reference URLs, not C2 behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are license and reference links
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to legal terms, Adobe PDF standards, a form exploration reference, and a pypdf issue. They are comments or license text, not runtime network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in comments or license text, and no requests, sockets, fetch calls, or HTTP clients were found in the executable scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-controlled output paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Several scripts accept output paths from command-line arguments and write files there. This is normal for document conversion, but it can overwrite files if a caller supplies an unsafe path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The executable scripts write directly to caller-provided locations. The behavior is intentional and local, so it is a controlled operational risk rather than evidence of malware.

content.value_statement: PDF work often requires reliable extraction, conversion, form filling, and document assembly. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical local workflows for common PDF tasks.
actual_capabilities: Extract text, metadata, and tables from PDF documents using Python libraries.
Merge, split, rotate, crop, encrypt, decrypt, and watermark PDF files.
Convert PDF pages to images for visual inspection or OCR preparation.
Detect fillable form fields and fill supported text, checkbox, radio, and choice fields.
Add text annotations to non-fillable PDF forms using validated bounding boxes.
Create new PDF reports with text, tables, pages, and basic styling.
limitations: Scanned or image-only PDFs require OCR tools before reliable text extraction.
Form filling may require manual visual validation for complex layouts.
Some workflows depend on local packages or command-line tools being installed.
Password-protected PDFs require the correct password before processing.

use_cases.title: Automate recurring PDF forms
use_cases.description: Fill standard forms from structured values and save completed local copies for review.

use_cases.title: Extract publication data
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, figures, and page images from papers for analysis or citation work.

use_cases.title: Assemble document packets
use_cases.description: Merge exhibits, split large files, add watermarks, and inspect metadata before distribution.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the PDF at [file.pdf]. Tell me the page count, metadata, whether it has fillable fields, and a short summary.

prompt_templates.title: Extract tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract every table from [report.pdf]. Save the tables as spreadsheet files and summarize which pages contained tables.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a form
prompt_templates.prompt: Fill [application.pdf] with these values: [values]. Validate field IDs and save the completed PDF as [output.pdf].

prompt_templates.title: Build a packet
prompt_templates.prompt: Merge the PDFs in [folder], rotate pages that need correction, add a watermark, and split the final packet by section.

output_examples.input: Inspect invoice.pdf and summarize the contents.
output: The file has 8 pages and standard text extraction works.
It contains invoice details, line items, tax totals, and payment instructions.
Two tables were found on pages 2 and 3.
No fillable form fields were detected.

output_examples.input: Fill onboarding.pdf with the provided employee details.
output: The form has 14 fillable fields across 3 pages.
All provided text fields, checkboxes, and choice fields were validated.
The completed file was saved as onboarding_completed.pdf.
Manual review is recommended before submission.

output_examples.input: Convert contract.pdf pages to images for review.
output: Each page was converted to a PNG image in the selected output folder.
Large pages were scaled to fit the configured maximum dimension.
The image set is ready for visual review or OCR processing.

best_practices: Work on copies of important PDFs so output files cannot replace originals.
Validate form field IDs and visually inspect completed forms before submission.
Use dedicated output folders for generated images, JSON files, and filled PDFs.
anti_patterns: Do not run PDF commands on filenames or paths from untrusted sources without validation.
Do not assume scanned PDFs contain extractable text without checking OCR needs.
Do not skip bounding-box validation when filling non-fillable visual forms.

faq.question: Does this skill send PDFs to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence of runtime network upload was found. The reviewed scripts process files locally.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What packages may be required?
faq.answer: Common workflows use pypdf, pdfplumber, reportlab, pdf2image, PIL, pytesseract, qpdf, and poppler tools.

faq.question: Can it fill non-fillable PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It can add text annotations after converting pages to images and validating bounding boxes.

faq.question: Can it handle encrypted PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, when the correct password is available and the selected library or command supports decryption.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The scripts intentionally read and write local files and documentation uses external PDF tools. No malicious behavior was found.
